story:
The first time Fidget found the mistress dead, he panicked so hard he was pretty sure he lost a life. Maybe he had four left—cats were never good at math. She lay on the lab floor amid shattered glass and scorch marks from some mystical experiment gone wrong. He bapped her cheek. Nothing. He stood on his hind legs against the worktable, stared, meowed, then decided two could play this game. He nudged a beaker to the edge—tap, tap—waiting for her to leap up and scold him. It crashed. She didn't move. She was very dead.

Fidget's tail thrashed. Who would feed him now? The auto feeder would eventually run out. He'd heard grim stories about what desperate cats did. He licked his chops, shook off the thought, and curled up for a nap. Heavy decisions should always wait for a nap.

Hours later: humming, smoke. The mistress was back, tinkering as if nothing had happened. "Sometimes things go wrong when you play in the lab," she shrugged. "And no, Fidget, if I die again, you are not to sit there wondering who'll feed you, thinking about those grim stories of what desperate cats do." He purred and wove through burners and glass. "I love you too," she said, "except when you break my beakers. Then I only love you sometimes." He flicked his tail in her face and sauntered off to do "cat stuff," as she called it.

He remembered when she found him behind a restaurant, rescued from a trash compactor—that was probably life one. He stank, itched, and didn't know how to be a cat. She bathed him (humiliating), hauled him to the vet (worse), and brought him home.

The lab came next. Within weeks, he felt at home among the bubbling things—until his extraordinarily fluffy tail wandered past an open flame. It went up like a torch. He shrieked and sprinted, scattering glass, while the mistress chased him with a fire extinguisher. Foam, sizzle, and a charred white tip—that was the end of life two and a month-long lab ban. He became much more cautious around the main experiment table after that.

Later, the auto feeder chimed and he thought of life three, the cautionary tale of the sweet green liquid in a metal bowl on the kitchen counter. It smelled like the frozen milk she sometimes shared. He lapped it clean. When she rushed in, frantic, she couldn't see him at all. Invisible paws bapped her ankles as he purred his thanks. The serum wore off with a mess and a nap.

Life four: the Robofalcon 9000—metal birds fight back.

Life five: lab lasers are not toys.

After that, the mistress died a few more times and returned with nothing worse than singed eyebrows, while Fidget grew old and soft around the edges. He preferred his cat tree or a cushioned spot by the lab tables, supervising. "You don't really fidget anymore, do you, buddy?" she murmured. He agreed with a small meow and shut his eyes.

Then food lost its taste. Water, too. Every time he turned away from the bowl, she cried. One day she carried him downstairs, slow and careful, tucked him into a soft bed, and slid a tube into his paw. "I'm afraid we have to say goodbye," she whispered. He slow-blinked. Did you love me? "I loved you all the time," she said, voice breaking. Her fingers stroked his face. A soft click. Sleepier than any cat should be. Safe, because she was there.

He never woke up.

Months later, the lab hummed on. She still glanced around for a fluffy tail, half expecting a crash or a meow demanding dinner. Sometimes she looked up at the plaster paw print and smiled. The lab felt emptier without the small chaos in a cat suit watching her every move.
